# Questions in Livestream 215

Live Stream #215: Amalgamated Lamentations

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=742" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:22</a> | Will the narrator and all characters be voice-acted by both Doctor and Producer Falk? | He said only Doctor Falk would do the voice acting because Producer Falk did not want to. | He answers the suggestion directly: the planned narration and character voices will be done by him alone. He says he asked Producer Falk, but she did not want to do it, and he adds that he is not a professional voice actor, so the result may be interesting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=801" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:21</a> | Which pharaoh would have reigned during Abraham's visit to Egypt? | He said we do not know, though he places Abraham roughly in the 13th Dynasty and mentions rulers around Neferhotep I as plausible. | He places Abraham's Egypt visit in the 13th Dynasty, but says the dynasty's unusually short reigns make a precise identification impossible. Because many kings ruled only a few years, the relevant pharaoh could be one of many rulers, though the link with Hammurabi and Neferhotep I narrows the range to kings near Neferhotep I rather than naming one securely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=906" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:06</a> | Why were the king's reigns so short in the 13th Dynasty, and were they overthrown by military or common people? | He said we do not know; rotation and assassination theories have been floated, but the evidence does not settle it. | He stresses that the reason for the short reigns is unknown. He mentions proposed explanations such as rotating kingship or assassinations, but does not endorse them, and says the surviving evidence mainly shows that many kings existed, left seals and names, and ruled from the same place without explaining why turnover was so high. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=1031" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:11</a> | If reign lengths were so short, what prevented the dynasty from quickly running out of heirs? | He said that may indeed have been part of the instability, but the evidence is too thin to answer confidently. | He treats running out of heirs as possible, but says there is not enough data to know what was happening. The limits of the evidence are that there were many 13th Dynasty kings, their reigns were exceptionally short, they ruled from the same place, and the dynasty left too little explanatory text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=1090" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:10</a> | What is the etymology of Asher? | He said the etymology is not secure and discussed possible links with Asherah-type forms, Asserta, and Osiris without treating any option as settled. | He begins with the blunt answer that the etymology is unknown. He says there may be relationships among Asher, the Levantine Asherah, Mesopotamian Asserta, and possibly Egyptian Assur or Osiris, including the shared use of a fetish-pole image, but he treats these as possible connections rather than a settled derivation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=1301" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:41</a> | Is the Ethiopian church heretical because it accepts Enoch and some difficult ideas? | He said no, preferring to describe some features as problematic or aberrant rather than church-destroying heresy. | He distinguishes heresy from aberrance. In his usage, heresy means a denial of a core Christian doctrine serious enough to amount to another gospel, another Jesus, another God, or a destructive corruption of humanity's place; by contrast, problematic teachings that do not rise to that level should be called aberrant, so he does not label the Ethiopian church heretical on this basis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=1627" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:07</a> | Are there restrictions on what counts as prayer in Christianity? | He said yes, because prayer can be misdirected, wrongly motivated, or undermined by persistent sinful posture. | He says some restrictions are obvious, such as praying to the wrong god, because that would not be Christian prayer. He also gives examples of disordered motives, such as praying for luxury or money out of pride, and later frames prayer as something that can be disqualified by sinful posture rather than merely by using religious language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=1989" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:09</a> | How can a person of faith recognize a calling? | He said calling may appear as an unmistakable passion, as a kicking-and-screaming pressure from God, or may simply not be clear yet. | He says a calling is usually a long-term dedication to ministry, profession, occupation, or lifestyle. Some people do not yet have one and need life experience, while others recognize it through an overwhelming passion they would pursue regardless of hardship; he also describes a reluctant calling where God closes other paths until the person is dragged into the work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=2513" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:53</a> | Why were humans less central in Egyptian mythology than in Genesis? | He said Egyptian mythology is more centered on gods, kingship, and cosmic order than on humanity as the climax of creation. | He explains that Egyptian creation myths do not consistently make human creation central, and some do not discuss it at all. By contrast, Genesis makes humanity the image and likeness of God, which he connects with idol language and the climax of creation; Egyptian myth lacks that same apex role because the gods themselves are many, mixed, animal-linked, and not arranged around humanity in the same way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=2815" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:55</a> | Did Israelites ever manufacture weapons on a significant scale? | He said yes, especially in periods when northern kingdom wealth was tied to horses, chariots, and military trade. | He says Israel's major military production was chariots and horse teams, especially in the northern kingdom after the division of the monarchy. He connects this to Israel's wealth, trade routes, Megiddo's later stables, and biblical references to exports involving Hittite and Aramean kings, while adding that outside this chariot trade Israel was not otherwise deeply in the arms race. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=3265" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:25</a> | What is the difference between an M.Div. and a Th.M.? | He said the M.Div. is the broad pastoral and ministry degree, while the Th.M. is usually a more advanced research-oriented follow-up, with Dallas using the labels differently. | He describes the M.Div. as a professional pastoral foundation covering Scripture, pastoral practice, theology, and biblical languages in many programs. The Th.M. is usually a one-year post-M.Div. research degree with a thesis, though he notes Dallas Theological Seminary uses the terms differently by making its Th.M. more like an M.Div. with Greek and Hebrew rather than a separate thesis-based research step. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=3581" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:41</a> | Was Ramesses II less powerful than his reputation suggests? | He said yes, portraying Ramesses as an impressive builder whose military record and practical dominance are often exaggerated. | He calls Ramesses II a great builder but a poor general, especially at Kadesh, where he says Ramesses came close to defeat and could not hold the city. He also argues Egyptian control in the Levant had already weakened because of Hittite conflict and Sea Peoples pressure, and notes that after campaigning early in his reign Ramesses stayed home for decades rather than acting like Thutmose III. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=3901" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:01</a> | Was the Concordia seminary he mentioned St. Louis or Fort Wayne? | He said it was Fort Wayne. | He answers the clarification directly: the Concordia he had in mind was Fort Wayne. The surrounding discussion concerns seminary language requirements and his own recollections of Concordia, but the identification itself is simply Fort Wayne. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=4004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:44</a> | Was the Sermon on the Mount the same event as the Sermon on the Plain? | He said probably not the same event, though the overlap in content is unsurprising if Jesus repeated themes or if the Evangelists arranged similar teaching differently. | He initially says he would need to compare the gospel synopsis line by line to answer with precision, then says the events are probably different. He cautions that different events can still reuse teaching material, so overlap between Matthew and Luke does not by itself prove they report the same occasion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=4578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:18</a> | Were the Hyksos Asiatic or Palestinian in origin? | He said they were Levantine Asiatic peoples, especially associated with Amorite-type backgrounds, not Palestinian in either the Philistine or modern Arab sense. | He identifies the Hyksos as Amorites, and therefore Asiatic in the ancient Egyptian sense because Amorites came from the Levant. He rejects calling them Palestinian, distinguishing them from Philistines as Sea Peoples and from modern Palestinians, neither of which matches the Amorite background he is describing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=4886" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:26</a> | Is there a big difference between East and West Coast Canadian accents? | He said yes, contrasting Newfoundland speech with Vancouver and noting that Vancouver sounds much closer to the U.S. West Coast. | He says the difference is substantial, using Newfoundland and Vancouver as his contrast. In his description, Vancouver speech is very close to the U.S. West Coast or California sound, while Newfoundland has a distinct flavor, including a more stereotypically eastern pronunciation of words like "about." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=5428" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:28</a> | What is your favorite ancient Near Eastern idiom? | He highlighted idioms such as cutting a covenant and the Mizpah formula that works more like a threat than a sentimental blessing. | He names "cut a treaty" or "cut a covenant" as one favorite, connecting it with the act of cutting a document or the ritual language around treaty-making. He also points to the Mizpah saying in Genesis 31, "May the Lord be between you and me," noting that it is often treated sentimentally today even though in context it functions as a warning or threat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=5910" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:30</a> | Why not make YouTube Shorts to attract more viewers? | He said shorts require a lot of work, have diminishing returns, and YouTube is no longer pushing them the way it once did. | He says Shorts used to be nearly required for channel growth because YouTube's algorithm was steering attention that way. In his view that has changed: Shorts take substantial work for declining return, and YouTube is now more interested in long-form commercial content from major outlets where it can sell more expensive ads. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=6529" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:49</a> | Are bad reviews acceptable? | He said yes, because honest mixed or negative reviews make the overall review profile look more trustworthy. | He tells viewers to be honest rather than intentionally negative or artificially positive. A bad review is acceptable if that is the reader's real reaction, and he says a mix of reviews can even make the profile look more credible because too many positive reviews can look automated or bought. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=6683" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:23</a> | Is Amazon the best place to get your books? | He said it is a good option, partly because Amazon's recommendation system helps discovery even if his direct financial return is similar elsewhere. | He says Amazon is a good way to buy the books, though not necessarily the only best way, because his return is similar either way. The main advantage is visibility: if Amazon sees sales and profit potential, it is more likely to recommend and promote the books, even though Amazon takes a large share. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=6760" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:40</a> | Is there a connection between the number of the beast and Solomon? | He said yes, reading 666 against Solomon's sudden concentrated wealth as part of the symbolic background for the beast figure. | He reads Revelation's 666 through Old Testament symbolism, especially 1 Kings 10 and Solomon receiving 666 talents of gold in a year. He argues Revelation is meant to unveil and summarize Old Testament prophecy rather than obscure it, so the number evokes a man suddenly transformed by concentrated wealth and power, which becomes part of the beast imagery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=7763" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:23</a> | Is it worth learning Hebrew to read the Bible in the original? | He said absolutely, because it removes one layer of obfuscation even though exegesis is still required. | He strongly encourages learning Hebrew if someone can do it, while acknowledging that language study is hard. Reading in the original does not eliminate the need for exegesis, but it removes a layer of translation and helps the reader know at least which words and forms are actually present. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=7830" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:30</a> | What Bible translation is best overall? | He said he often uses the NASB 1977 for apologetics and especially likes the CSB as a balanced general choice. | He says he still uses the 1977 NASB for apologetic work because of its formal-equivalence attention to syntax and grammar. He also praises the Christian Standard Bible because it tries to balance formal and dynamic equivalence, paying attention to grammar and lexicography while still communicating the meaning clearly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=8003" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:23</a> | Are Bible translations shaped by commercial motives? | He said yes, because publishers often profit from the translation itself and from the broader ecosystem of study materials built on it. | He says commercial motives are a major reason North America has so many Bible translations, including some he views as unnecessary. A successful translation can generate money not only from Bibles but from commentaries, study materials, and licensing fees paid by authors and publishers who quote or build resources around that translation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=8148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:48</a> | If someone quotes the NIV in a public article, will they get sued? | He said no for a single quote or a couple of lines, but longer use in books can run into translation licensing limits. | He says a single quote or a couple of lines is not the problem. The issue is extended use: many Bible editions state quotation limits or permission rules in the front matter, and using large portions in a book or similar publication may require attention to the translation's licensing provisions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=8406" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:06</a> | What are some ways to improve D&D? | He said he would rather go back a few editions and allow more imaginative freedom than what he sees in the current direction. | He answers by criticizing the current edition and saying he would go back a few editions. His preferred improvement is to let players and creators be more imaginative, and he frames recent changes to creatures and moral categories as a move away from what he thinks made earlier D&D work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=8576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:56</a> | Weren't orcs supposed to be the evil faction originally in D&D? | He said yes, in older D&D they were evil, but they have since been rehabilitated in strange ways. | He says that in older D&D, orcs were indeed treated as evil. His broader point is that later versions have rehabilitated them, which he treats as part of the same shift he had just criticized in current D&D design. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=8620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:40</a> | Is Paul Overland credible? | He said he does not know the person and therefore cannot judge. | He gives no evaluation because he does not recognize the name. Since he has never heard of Paul Overland, he says he cannot say whether the person is credible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=8665" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:25</a> | What is your take on the Tower of Babel in Genesis 11:1-9? | He treated it as mythohistory, meaning a historical core is being interpreted through a theological and literary frame. | He defines mythohistory as history that has been mythologized or lifted into religious interpretation, not pure fiction and not bare chronicle. For Babel, he says the likely historical core is the building of a ziggurat, with the story interpreting that event theologically around humanity's refusal to fill the earth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=8919" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:39</a> | Does mythohistory mean people take history and ask what the event says about spiritual reality? | He said that is broadly the idea: mythohistory relates a historical core to a culture's understanding of divine or spiritual meaning. | He agrees that the question captures the gist. In his explanation, mythohistory asks how people reconcile a historical event with their understanding of divine action or spiritual reality, so it is a way of interpreting history through a religious worldview. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=8994" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:54</a> | Did pharaohs believe they would reincarnate into other earthly bodies after death? | He said no, because Egyptian afterlife belief is better described as cyclical continuation in the next world rather than reincarnation into new earthly bodies. | He says Egyptian pharaohs did not believe in reincarnation. They expected an eternally cyclical afterlife, especially traveling with Ra across the sky and through the underworld day after day; other Egyptians had their own afterlife roles, but this was a definite afterlife rather than returning in new bodies on earth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=9040" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:40</a> | Does Freemasonry come from ancient Egypt or Solomon's temple? | He said no; despite Masonic claims, he traces it to European guild movements rather than ancient Egypt or Solomon's temple. | He rejects the ancient Egyptian and Solomon's temple origin claims as Masonic self-presentation rather than history. He traces Freemasonry instead to European guild movements, especially stone-cutting guilds, describing them as medieval organizations connected with cathedral building and employment protection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=9188" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:08</a> | How did Masons turn into what they are now if they began as ordinary guilds? | He said the decline of cathedral-building work left stone-cutting guilds to become broader employment and networking organizations. | He says the stone-cutting work dried up, especially after the Protestant Reformation reduced cathedral building. The guilds survived by arranging other work for their networks, so he describes Freemasonry's later shape as an unintended development from medieval labor and employment structures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=9397" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:37</a> | Can someone be both a Christian and a Mason? | He said no, because Masonry allows multiple paths to God and is therefore incompatible with Christian exclusivity. | He says Christianity is incompatible with Masonry because Masonry presents religions as facets pointing toward truth, while Christianity claims Christ himself is the truth and the only way to the Father. He also objects to Masonic ideas of salvation through secret knowledge, which he compares to gnosticism rather than Christian faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=9703" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:43</a> | Could Freemasonry be rooted in Satan worship? | He said no, locating its roots more in Hermetic and occult currents than in explicit Satanism. | He says Freemasonry is not based on Satan worship. He locates its roots in Hermetic and broader Western occult thought during the Enlightenment period, while distinguishing that from Satanism, which he describes as self-worship or elitism rather than the basis of Masonic thought. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=10197" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:57</a> | If being a Freemason is supposed to be secret, could someone get in trouble for talking openly about membership? | He said generally no; they recruit openly, though they do not disclose the deeper secrets to outsiders. | He says open membership talk is generally not the issue because Freemasonry is always recruiting and members have to tell people something in order to recruit them. The secrecy concerns deeper rites, symbols, and higher-level teachings, not merely admitting that one is a Mason. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=10319" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:59</a> | Can anyone join Freemasonry, or do they prefer certain people? | He said they tend to be selective and somewhat elitist rather than universally open. | He says access depends on region and social context, but generally describes Masonic membership as elitist. They tend to prefer people with business, industry, or social connections and expect someone to look respectable, while formal statements about moral character and belief in God may set a much lower and vaguer bar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=10501" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:01</a> | Is Genesis 2:4 a recap chiasm of Genesis 1 or a different creation story? | He said neither, preferring to read it as a toledot-style transition that introduces the garden narrative rather than a second independent creation account. | He says Genesis 2:4 uses the toledot formula, which functions like a genealogical or structural transition. In his reading, Genesis 1:1 through 2:4 is the preamble or toledot, and Genesis 2:5 through chapter 3 is the garden narrative that follows, so it is not a chiasm and not two separate creation accounts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=10903" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:43</a> | How is AI in archaeology or Dead Sea Scroll work different from earlier computer analysis? | He said earlier computer methods already did important work, and current AI mostly helps adjudicate between other lines of evidence rather than replacing them. | He says computer analysis in the Dead Sea Scrolls and digital humanities has existed for decades, including pattern matching and handwriting or authorship work. The newer AI approach is different mostly in combining scholarly judgment, carbon-14 dating, and computer analysis to adjudicate dates, though he warns that constraining AI between those inputs may simply lead it toward the answer it was told to find. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=11315" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:35</a> | What kind of sauce works best with pasta? | He said it depends on the pasta and mentions options like carbonara, butter-garlic, Alfredo, and tomato sauces. | He says the best sauce depends on the pasta and what someone wants to eat. He lists carbonara, simple butter and garlic or parsley, Alfredo for some filled pastas, and tomato sauce cooked for a rich flavor, treating the choice as a matter of pairing rather than naming one universal best sauce. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=11461" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:01</a> | Are Pop-Tarts a ravioli? | He said no, because Pop-Tarts are pastries and are not boiled. | He says Pop-Tarts may look like ravioli, but they are pastries rather than pasta. The decisive distinction in his answer is that ravioli is boiled, while a Pop-Tart is not, so the category comparison fails. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=11519" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:59</a> | Are breakfast dessert pizzas a yes or no? | He said it depends, and he has had good apple strudel and strawberry dessert pizzas. | He gives a conditional yes, saying it depends on the dessert pizza. He remembers an apple strudel pizza as delicious and also liked a strawberry version with sliced strawberries on crust and a sugar crumble, though he frames them as desserts rather than health food. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=11890" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:10</a> | Which era of Saturday morning cartoons did you grow up with? | He said mostly the 1970s and early 1980s. | He names shows such as Dungeons and Dragons, old Scooby-Doo, Battle of the Planets, and the tail end of Robotech as examples of his cartoon era. He summarizes that as mostly the 1970s and very early 1980s. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=12220" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:40</a> | Why was Israel technologically behind its neighbors? Was that so it would rely on God? | He rejected the premise, saying Israel was a pastoral society and not uniquely backward in the simplistic way the question suggests. | He says Israel was not simply technologically backward, and asks what kind of technology the question has in mind. He describes ancient Israel as an Iron Age pastoral and farming society, not an ignorant one, and notes that cities in any society concentrate more technology than the countryside. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=12341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:41</a> | Do we really know how ancient Egyptian music sounded? | He said no, since proposed scales exist but the actual tuning, notation, and sound remain uncertain. | He says we have some instruments and can infer possibilities, such as a five-note scale, but cannot know the exact sound. The problem is that surviving instruments are not in tune, no Egyptian music survives in a written notation that can be played securely, and modern reconstructions remain interpretations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=12832" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:52</a> | How can students avoid brain rot and procrastination? | He said the two problems differ, with procrastination often tied to depression and brain rot tied to intellectual passivity, so the remedy is more real reading, thinking, and active engagement. | He separates procrastination from brain rot: procrastination may be tied to depression, self-regulation, or other psychological problems, while brain rot means mental faculties and attention have atrophied. His remedy is to stop offloading thought, recover the habit of sustained reading, and rebuild the capacity to engage whole books and real arguments rather than only snippets. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=13861" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:01</a> | When starting a new topic, should I buy one book or many? | He said to begin with one strong overview book and then follow its bibliography outward. | He recommends starting with one good general book on the subject instead of buying many at once. After reading it, use the bibliography as the next book list, because good books usually contain built-in paths for drilling deeper into a field. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/LYoh2N5SRJQ?t=14038" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:58</a> | Is there a good way to know whether a book has a useful bibliography before buying it? | He said yes, by using previews or library access and then judging whether the citations are current and substantial. | He suggests checking Amazon's preview, Google Scholar snippets, library copies, or conference book tables to inspect the bibliography before buying. To judge usefulness, he looks for substantial and current sources, warning that a bibliography dominated by very old works may be a poor guide unless the field or topic specifically requires them. |
